figures.caption: Fig. 2. Average latency (seconds) versus TP/PP configuration for four model architectures: Qwen1.5-MoE (blue), Mamba-130m (orange), Mistral7B (green), and NVIDIA Nemotron-Nano-9B (red). Dense and MoE models achieve 60–64% latency reductions from TP = 1 to TP = 4, while Mamba130m remains largely insensitive due to its cache-free recurrent architecture.

Fig. 3. Average energy (Joules) versus TP/PP configuration for four model architectures. Energy trends largely follow latency, with all large models achieving 67–71% reductions at (TP=4, PP=1). Crucially, unlike latency, pipeline parallelism provides measurable energy savings even for dense transformers. Mamba-130m maintains 10–20× lower energy across all configurations.

Fig. 1. Overview of the ENERGYLENS training pipeline. The procedure consists of five stages: (1) definition of the input space, comprising configuration parameters, families of models, GPU architectures, and workload types; (2) systematic energy profiling of multimodal LLM serving across diverse models and configurations, producing reusable datasets; (3) symbolic-regression-driven model construction that discovers closed-form energy formulas from profiling data; (4) optimization of ENERGYLENS by identifying Pareto-optimal configurations from the learned energy function Energy = f(max_tokens,tp,pp, . . .) after having it being fitted on a small set of configs profiled; and (5) deployment, yielding optimal configurations for known setups and energy profiles for unseen configurations.

Fig. 4. Average energy (Joules) versus TP/PP configuration for Mistral-7B across Intel Aurora, AMD MI100, and NVIDIA A100. AMD achieves the lowest absolute energy; NVIDIA benefits most from higher TP degrees; Intel exhibits a non-monotone response to PP scaling.

Fig. 5. Average per-inference energy (Joules) versus batch size for Qwen7B across TP/PP configurations. All configurations exhibit steep energy decay at small batch sizes, converging to ∼5-10 J beyond batch size 50. Higher parallelism achieves lower energy at all batch sizes.

Fig. 6. Average energy (Joules) versus TP/PP configuration for Qwen-7B under three workload modalities. Video + text is substantially more expensive at baseline; all modalities benefit from parallelism, but modality gaps persist even at high TP.

TABLE VII CROSS-BATCH-SIZE EXTRAPOLATION: MODELS TRAINED ON THE SMALLEST 40% OF BATCH SIZES, EVALUATED ON THE REMAINING LARGER BATCH SIZES. PAIR ACC” IS PAIRWISE RANKING ACCURACY (%); REGRET” IS MEAN ENERGY OVERHEAD (%) VS. ORACLE. BOLD MARKS THE BETTER EXTRAPOLATED RESULT PER ROW.

Fig. 8. Pairwise accuracy without per-configuration power measurements. ENERGYLENS models energy directly and maintains high accuracy across all text and multimodal tasks. In contrast, MAVERIQ, when restricted to a mean power proxy, suffers widespread degradation. Models: M1 = LLaVA1.5-7B-image-chat, M2 = Mistral-7B, M3 = NVIDIA-Nemotron-V2-9B, M4 = Qwen1.5-MoE-2.7B, M5 = Qwen2-VL-7B-image-chat, M6 = Qwen2VL-7B-text, M7 = Qwen2-VL-7B-video-chat, M8 = Qwen2-VL-7B-video-only.
TABLE I COMPARISON OF ENERGYLENS WITH REPRESENTATIVE PRIOR ENERGY AND PERFORMANCE MODELS FOR LLM INFERENCE. ✓/✗ INDICATE WHETHER THE PROPERTY IS SUPPORTED.

Fig. 7. Average energy (Joules) versus TP/PP configuration for Qwen2-VL-7B at BF16, 4-bit, and 8-bit quantisation. Counterintuitively, 4-bit raises energy at most configurations; 8-bit offers the most robust advantage.
